sentences.text: My name is Harry Brill.
B-R-I-L-L.
At home, at my childhood, they used to call me Herschel.
My Yiddish name is Herschel.
I was born in Brody, Poland, which is [INAUDIBLE].
I was born on March the 10th, 1930.
65 years old.
I remember that I had three sisters, and I was the only boy.
We were four children.
And I remember from the age of five-- I remember when my father died-- 1935.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then, I remember my family-- my mother took care of the house.
She never remarried.
She took care of the business.
My sister-- my older sister, my middle sister used to help in the business and in the-- and in the house.
My mother was in the beer distribution, and also they owned a grocery store-- a wholesale grocery store.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lived not far from the business.
I used to visit a lot, the business.
No, I was very young.
I was very young.
Yeah.
I was the youngest, and what I remember is when my father died, I used to go to a afternoon Jewish school, which they call it a cheder.
And I used to have a man who used to come every afternoon and take me to the synagogue to say the Kaddish and take me home every day.
And for holidays-- for the high holidays or for any holidays, I used to celebrate at my uncle's home, which was very, very religious man.
I used to go with him to the synagogue and spend with him some holidays together till the-- till the-- till the war broke out.
Sure.
Yeah, I had my uncle and aunt, which was my father's sister.
I had cousins.
I had uncles.
I had-- we had-- a few-- a few kilometers away, we had some relatives that used to come and visit.
And I remember it all, yeah.
My father died of a heart attack.
I couldn't tell you exactly, but it was in the late 30s.
From what I read and what I remember, what I read after the war, that the population was around 10,000 Jews.
The total population of Brody, according to what I read, is around 30,000 people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's after the war, what I read.
And we lived in a Jewish neighborhood.
I played with Jewish kids.
I never had any gentile friends, never integrated.
I don't remember.
I-- I I wouldn't remember if my mother did business with other people besides Jews.
I-- I couldn't tell you, but probably yes.
But I couldn't remember the people.
I started school at the age of seven.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went till the age of nine.
And it was a Jewish-- it was not a religious school, but it was a Jewish-- Jewish regular school.
In the afternoon, after I finished school, I used to go to the cheder.
This was strictly Hebrew-- Hebrew and Torah, you know.
Going to school-- sometime as I grew up, you had to watch yourself.
You didn't cross out in the streets.
You had to go in your regular path.
If you went out to a gentile neighborhood, you could-- you could get beaten up sometimes.
So it was-- you had to learn from childhood how to survive.
No.
You know, what-- hassled, you know?
Sometimes I was hassled around, called names or thrown stones between kids, but never beaten up badly or stuff like that.
The anti-Semitic thing that I remember is you knew you were Jewish, and you had to watch yourself.
You don't mingle with-- with Gentiles.
There were a lot of Ukrainian that lived in our town and Polish, and you just stayed in-- in your side.
What I remember about dangerous is I remember we had a radio.
And when-- when-- when Hitler used to talk in '37 or '38, people used to surround themselves and listen, and they used to talk.
As much as I understood, they used to talk about the danger, but thinking that it'll never happen to them.
Ah, it's only-- you know, it's going to pass by and--
I mean, more freedom than-- than the other people had on the German side.
And as I told you, in June, broke out the war, and 10 days later came the Germans to town, and we were occupied by German people.
And then started all the danger.
And we know what's happening, yeah.
I personally didn't know a big difference between Polish and Ukrainian, but I know there were villages-- Ukrainian villages.
There were-- there were Polish villages.
It was mixed.
What I learned after that, that there were tense relations between Ukrainian and Polish people, too.
How the Jewish population-- what drew them together or mixed them-- I don't remember.
I lived in Brody on [POLISH].
I was born on [POLISH].
And then we lived on [POLISH], and our business was on that street.
My mother's business.
[POLISH] is the-- the railroad street, the railroad street.
Well, go onto that street about probably two, three miles, that was the railroad.
That was the rail.
Right.
OK, the activity started when the Russian came, and a lot of Jews, who ran away from the German side to our side, were picked up by Russian, put on trains, and they took them to Siberia.
And my mother was very active by helping them, collecting bread and going to the railroad station.
However, when the Russian came, immediately a few months later, they evicted us from the house because of businesses.
People who own businesses or stores-- they were considered as capitalists.
So the Russians evicted them.
We had to go to live in a little house, and that's the time when we moved from that house.
So and the German occupation, the activity that happened, I was not near the-- the railroad.
OK.
What happened is when the German came, the first 10 days of the occupation, the usual-- they took in our city, doctors, business people.
They arrested them.
Among those people were my mother and two sisters.
I was with a friend.
We played.
And my sister was with a girlfriend, so we weren't caught.
However, somebody came to me and told me, go fast home.
Something is happening.
The Germans are in-- in your little-- when I came, my mother and my two sisters were already in the car, and they waved to me to disappear.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So this happened the first 10 days.
[SIGH]
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were taken-- well, we were left alone.
So we were taken to-- to my aunt and uncle.
And we started living with them, because we didn't have any parents.
The first three days-- see, Jews were marked up by-- by a yellow star-- by a band on-- on their arm.
But from a certain age-- you know, because I was only 11 years old, I could survive on the streets and see what was going on.
No, I didn't-- wear a band.
It was from a certain age.
I couldn't remember.
So what I did is-- my mother was taken to a jail, and from a jail-- daytime-- to a high school, which was the Gestapo house, for questioning.
I used to be across the street on the top of a roof, and I used to watch.
They were questioned.
They were beaten up.
They were pulled by motorcycles, you know?
And every night, back to the jail.
This happened for three days, and I used to-- I used to follow the truck.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the fourth day, on the fourth evening, after the right turn, there was a left.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they took them to a field-- [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were killed.
It was a truckload of people, and my mother's group was probably around 35 people.
I was standing on the corner of the street.
And when they took that left turn, after going to jail on the right turn-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
She saw me, and she was screaming and crying.
Well, that was it.
Yes.
So I lived with my uncle and aunt, me and my sister, yeah.
Yes, yes.
It was a large family.
I think there were-- they had three daughters and four sons.
And my uncle was not a well-to-do man.
He had a small factory of candles.
And-- but it was held by his sons that went to work for Germans.
And it was tough.
It was tough.
It was quite tough to live on.
Yes.
Yes.
They were-- the youngest daughter was probably about three years older than me, and then-- then the sons were probably five and six, seven and 10 years older than me, yes.
School was not allowed.
In the beginning, when the Germans came, my uncle tried still to put me in underground, in cheder, you know?
It was very dangerous if they caught you, so my cousins persuaded him that that's enough.
And but it only lasted a few months, then it became illegal.
No schooling.
No, Jews were not allowed to go to any school.
Well, we lived-- since my mother was taken, we lived there till the end, till we were taken to the-- to the ghetto.
And it was from 1941 till around December 1942, they opened the ghetto.
Well, mostly yes.
Sometimes the sons disappeared to work in a certain area, but then they showed up again.
Matter of fact, one of my-- one of her sons, which was my cousin, organized a-- a little group, a partisan group.
They went to the woods, and they tried to do this.
But they showed up because their parents were there and their brothers and sisters.
So we were together, living in-- in the ghetto in two little rooms.
And in 1943-- in 1943, around May-- they declared Judenfrei, which is--
Which you probably know about Judenfrei.
And we had a bunker under the floor, in a basement in the same building.
We were prepared, you know?
In the ghetto, we had the little--
If-- if i can-- if I remember before the ghetto, when I lived with my uncle and aunt, that was also in the basement.
Because, you see, the Germans used to have what they call actions.
They used to-- like 3,000 Jews they used to grab to concentration camps, working camps, like men, you know?
When this happened, you used to hide.
And this usually took between a day and three days, and then we used to come out and get on again with the-- with our regular life.
And this, I think, happened in our city three times till they declared that I have to-- we have to go and go live in the ghetto.
The Germans-- the German Army, when they marched to Russia, exchanged-- come back from Russia-- I remember they used to have on the streets orchestras.
And they gave shows for the Army by catching Jews.
Shave their heads, beat them up.
This was their entertainment.
Me as a child, by not wearing any marks, I could be there and watch it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The ghetto lasted from December-- like I told you-- until probably May.
I celebrated my bar mitzvah in a small, little house.
It was only 10 people.
It was two rooms.
We must have lived maybe-- maybe 12 people, 14 people.
Two little rooms, yeah.
And--
Well, food was rationed by German.
Me and my sister had a lot of stuff left over from my mother.
When they took my mother, we took a lot of stuff.
My older sister was engaged.
And in Poland, there was a-- a habit when somebody got married, the mother used to prepare for her, you know-- bedspreads and pillow sheets and-- and we had a lot of stuff left from my mother.
Me and my sister used to sell, once in a while, some merchandise and help out.
To live, because my aunt and uncle were not economically well done.
The only thing that the sons used to work for German-- for the border police.
And they used to give him home certain pieces meat what they used to slaughter, you know?
But my uncle wouldn't touch it, because he was strictly kosher.
And he was going on.
So you know, we used to-- we used to help out.
So me and my sister had a little bit of merchandise.
Whatever you could, you used to sell and try to survive, that was-- you did whatever you could.
No, the only thing you could go is-- I saw some people collecting poison ivy and cook it and eat it.
There were Jewish people walking around swollen from hunger.
No, in ghetto, there was no-- no possibilities to steal-- from whom?
From you to you?
I mean, there was nothing left.
No, no, I never went out from the ghetto.
Never.
Never snuck out from the ghetto, no.
And when the Judenfrei broke out, we went into the underground.
I mean, in-- in the basement.
And we prepared rain water outside from the rain, from the gutters in liters.
And once a week, Saturday night, they used to open up the bricks-- there's an opening-- go out and bring in as much water as you could.
Oh, no.
Every week to get the water, right.
Otherwise, I stayed in that bunker.
Now, my sister was taken to a labor camp in Brody, and she worked.
Now, a lot of the people who worked in the camp knew where Jews were hidden.
The German used to use them to clean up the furniture and all the wealth of the Jews in the houses and put it in-- in a certain place in Brody.
The people who used to be in the camp used to throw through the chimney-- there was a chimney going down to the basement-- used to throw us in food.
And then we prepared before grains, you know, mostly things that-- it was rationed, you know?
I mean, we used to get the--
I would say close to 40 people.
It was very crowded, yeah.
Sometimes not enough air.
I remember I used to put my face on the ground to get air.
Sometimes there was not even enough air.
And people got sick.
I got the typhus in the bunker.
I was very sick-- high fever, but I came out of it.
Some people died.
We buried them right under, you know, in the ground.
And my sister apparently, with a friend, survived.
We had a castle in town where a princess used to live.
And the Germans used to bring the furniture, the furniture to that stuff and store it.
From the Jewish homes.
And her boyfriend used to work there.
So apparently, she survived with him over there.
The Commander-- the Polish Commander of the Jewish camp, which was a [INAUDIBLE] That means his ancestors from somewhere-- they were-- they were German.
They were special treated as Germans.
He was the Commandant of the camp, and he must have killed a lot of Jews.
But probably to justify him or to save himself, and for money, too, he made one room in that castle with the princess who-- the rumor was that he was in love with her and for money.
And they build one room.
They closed it up with bricks outside and they boarded up, and he kept 20-some Jews.
So my sister was in that group, and after she got herself settled in that room, she sent two men at night.
At that Saturday when we went out for the water, they came, took me to her place, and we joined together.
Oh, yeah.
We were notified by a message through the chimney.
Now, a few days before I left, we knew already that-- in the beginning, the German used to clean up the house, then they start searching for people.
So we heard them already searching, and we knew less and less and less food is coming in.
And then we had messages through pieces of paper that-- that so much they can help, and the camp is disappearing.
And I was notified to wait.
That Saturday when they open up to go for water, I will be taken to my sister.
So I came there.
They washed me up, and then they put me in another room.
So I was with my sister, and it was one room.
We all lived in one room.
And once a day--
I was the youngster.
I was only 13 and a half.
Mostly were people over 18, because they couldn't work in the camp, because they were less than 18.
So I was the only child.
Mostly there were people from-- from 19 to-- to probably to late-- mid-40s, something.
Probably those people work in the camp, and they made arrangement through-- you know, one probably knows the other, and through that-- through that Commandant from the camp.
He-- and whoever had money paid.

My sister had a bag of gold coins that our mother left it, because-- you see, the Jews in-- in Poland-- probably in the other countries-- if they made any money, they cashed it in for gold-- for gold coins.
So if they have ever to run, they'll survive.
